target.title: Add QA evidence summary normalization
target.body:
## Summary

- Add a normalized QA evidence summary schema grouped by `test`, `mapping`, `execution`, and `result` so suite, live transport, Vitest, and Playwright proof can share one artifact shape without forcing everything into scenario terminology.
- Keep taxonomy and mapping provenance out of the base evidence producer: evidence stores runnable-test identity, mapping relation IDs/refs, execution facts, artifacts, and result data; report/gate consumers join that through the checked-in taxonomy and mapping layers.
- Treat evidence `profile` as an opaque mapping id in the artifact. The mapped runner/taxonomy stack validates runnable profile membership; the base evidence schema does not hardcode the profile list.
- Embed the foundational `evidence` block in QA suite and Telegram live summaries without adding a release gate.

## Verification

- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/qa-lab/src/evidence-summary.test.ts extensions/qa-lab/src/suite.summary-json.test.ts extensions/qa-lab/src/suite-summary.test.ts extensions/qa-lab/src/live-transports/telegram/telegram-live.runtime.test.ts`
- `git diff --check origin/main...HEAD`
- `git diff --check`
- live PR diff/body check for personal-name owner references

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: QA Lab outputs now include a normalized evidence summary shape that can represent QA suite scenarios, live transport checks, Vitest tests, and Playwright tests.

Real environment tested: Local OpenClaw Codex worktree on macOS, with focused unit/schema tests.

Exact steps or command run after this patch: Ran the focused evidence summary, suite summary JSON, suite summary rendering, and Telegram live runtime tests through `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs`, plus whitespace and live PR diff/body checks.

Evidence after fix: Focused Vitest passed 4 files / 65 tests; `git diff --check origin/main...HEAD` passed; `git diff --check` passed; the live PR diffs and bodies contain no personal-name owner references.

Observed result after fix: Evidence entries are grouped as `test`, `mapping`, `execution`, and `result`. Mapping coverage rows keep relation role, surface/category IDs, and ref IDs; refs are normalized once under `mapping.refs`; execution contains provider/model, channel, runner, package source, environment, and artifact objects; result contains status, failure, and timing.

What was not tested: No maturity or release gate was added or run, no channel-driver SDK lane was implemented, and no live Telegram provider lane was executed for this scoped schema/output-shape change.

target.title: fix(plugins): load dreaming engine when memory slot plugin omitted from allowlist
target.body:
## Summary

- **Problem:** When a third-party Dreaming-compatible memory plugin holds the memory slot and `plugins.allow` is restrictive (omitting `memory-core`, the default dreaming engine), the dreaming engine is rejected as `not-in-allowlist` and never loads, so dreaming sweeps silently never run.
- **Why now:** Reported in #92536 with a source-level repro; affects any user who locks down `plugins.allow` while running dreaming on a non-default memory plugin.
- **Intended outcome:** The resolved dreaming engine activates as a sidecar of the selected memory slot even under a restrictive allowlist, while every explicit opt-out (`deny`, `entries.<id>.enabled: false`, workspace default-off) still wins.
- **Out of scope:** No new config/default surface, no change to how dreaming is configured, no gateway-startup changes (that path already includes the dreaming ids unconditionally — see below).
- **Reviewer focus:** Placement of the exemption in `resolvePluginActivationDecisionShared` relative to the deny/disabled/workspace gates, and whether the sibling gateway-startup path needs a parallel change (argued: no).

The dreaming engine is a runtime dependency of the selected memory slot. The fix adds a narrow `dreaming-engine-sidecar` exemption in the shared activation decision and threads the already-derived `dreamingEngineId` through the activation wrappers to the loader. The existing memory-slot guards (`pluginId !== dreamingEngineId`) already keep `memory-core` from claiming the slot, so it loads strictly as the sidecar.

## Linked context

Closes #92536

Was this requested by a maintainer or owner? No — found via issue triage.

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- **Behavior or issue addressed:** `memory-core` (the dreaming engine) is wrongly disabled (`not-in-allowlist`) when a restrictive `plugins.allow` omits it while a third-party plugin holds the memory slot with dreaming enabled.
- **Real environment tested:** Driven through the real plugin loader `loadOpenClawPlugins` (`src/plugins/loader.ts`) against on-disk synthetic bundled memory plugins (`memory-core` + `memory-lancedb`), Node 22+, `OPENCLAW_BUNDLED_PLUGINS_DIR` pointing at the fixtures — i.e. the actual activation/registry pipeline, not a mock.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:**
  - Reproduce the broken state first: revert only the three source files, keep the new test, run
    `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/plugins/loader.test.ts -t "enforces memory slot loading rules"`
  - Then with the fix applied, run the same command plus
    `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/plugins/channel-plugin-ids.test.ts`
- **Evidence after fix (terminal capture):**

  Before the fix (source reverted, config `allow: ["memory-lancedb"]`, dreaming on, `memory-core` omitted):
  ```
  × enforces memory slot loading rules
  AssertionError: expected 'disabled' to be 'loaded' // Object.is equality
  Expected: "loaded"
  Received: "disabled"
  ```
  After the fix:
  ```
  Test Files  1 passed (1)
       Tests  160 passed (160)   # src/plugins/loader.test.ts
       Tests  144 passed (144)   # src/plugins/channel-plugin-ids.test.ts
  ```
- **Observed result after fix:** The loader reports `memory-core` `status: "loaded"` (as a sidecar; `memorySlotSelected !== true`) while `memory-lancedb` remains the selected memory slot — so the dreaming engine is now present in the registry under a restrictive allowlist.
- **What was not tested:** End-to-end on a live gateway with a real third-party Dreaming-compatible plugin and an observed sweep-schedule readout (`openclaw memory status --deep`).
- **Proof limitations or environment constraints:** No real third-party memory plugin + LLM credentials available locally, so the live sweep-schedule readout was not exercised. The bug lives in the plugin-activation layer, which the loader-level before/after exercises directly; the gateway-startup path that schedules sweeps already includes the dreaming ids (`resolveGatewayStartupDreamingPluginIds` in `src/plugins/gateway-startup-plugin-ids.ts`) so it only depended on the loader enabling `memory-core`.
- **Before evidence:** Included above (the reverted-source failure).

## Tests and validation

Commands run:
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/plugins/loader.test.ts` → 160 passed
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/plugins/channel-plugin-ids.test.ts` → 144 passed
- `pnpm tsgo:core` and `pnpm tsgo:core:test` → no errors
- `oxlint` on the changed files → no findings; `oxfmt` → already formatted; `git diff --check` → clean

Regression coverage added: a characterization scenario in `src/plugins/loader.test.ts` ("loads dreaming engine when a restrictive allowlist omits it but dreaming is enabled") that fails before this fix (`memory-core` `disabled`) and passes after.

What failed before: that new scenario, with `expected 'disabled' to be 'loaded'`.

Note: I could not run `codex review` (the local Codex CLI hit its usage limit), so it was substituted with the typecheck/lint/format checks and an independent code review above.

## Risk checklist

- **User-visible behavior change?** Yes — the dreaming engine now activates (and dreaming sweeps run) for configs that previously silently skipped it. No change for configs where `memory-core` is explicitly denied/disabled, dreaming is off, or the slot is the default engine.
- **Config, environment, or migration behavior change?** No — no new config keys, defaults, or migrations.
- **Security, auth, secrets, network, or tool execution behavior change?** No.
- **Highest-risk area:** Loosening the allowlist gate for one plugin id. Mitigated by scoping the exemption strictly to the resolved `dreamingEngineId` (only ever the bundled `memory-core`, and only when dreaming is enabled with a non-default slot plugin — see `resolveDreamingSidecarEngineId`), and by placing it *after* the deny/disabled/workspace gates so explicit opt-outs still win.

## Current review state

Next action: maintainer review. Nothing blocking on the author. No bot/reviewer comments yet to address.
